skill.name: database
skill.description: Universal database operations skill for modern applications. Expert in SQLModel and SQLAlchemy patterns, async database operations, connection pooling, migrations, performance optimization, and multi-database support.
skill.summary: Guides teams through async database design, SQLAlchemy patterns, migrations, indexing, testing, and production database configuration.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: The static high-risk results are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, SQL text, and method names such as execute. No prompt injection, network exfiltration, install hook, or malicious runtime behavior was found, but database URL handling and raw SQL examples need publication warnings.

medium_findings.title: Database Connection String Handling in Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The examples read DATABASE_URL and include a fallback database URL with placeholder credentials. This is legitimate guidance, but users who copy it unchanged could expose credentials or connect to an unintended database.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable and connection string are directly present in the documented code. The impact is moderate because this is Markdown guidance, not executable skill code.

medium_findings.title: Raw SQL Diagnostic Query Example
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The performance section shows raw SQL passed to db.execute for PostgreSQL statistics. The query is fixed and not exfiltration, but production users should use bound parameters, SQLAlchemy text objects, and least-privilege monitoring roles.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The raw SQL execution pattern is visible and relevant to database security. It is not clearly exploitable because the shown query is static and the threshold is passed separately.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alerts point to Markdown metadata, SQL terms, and audit text rather than cryptographic code. No evidence found of weak hash algorithms, cipher use, password hashing, or token generation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced files are documentation and generated audit output, not crypto implementations. The reviewed context contains database terms and Markdown content only.

low_findings.title: Process Execution Static Hits Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The Python exec and process execution alerts map to database method names, query execution examples, and Markdown code fences. No evidence found of Python exec(), subprocess, shell commands, backtick shell execution, or command injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are part of database API examples. There is no shell or interpreter invocation in the reviewed Markdown.

low_findings.title: Local SQLite File Path Example
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The development example uses a local SQLite database file path. This is normal for development, but copied production code should avoid placing database files in unsafe working directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SQLite file path is explicitly present. The risk is low because it is labeled as development configuration and does not access files by itself.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Hits Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The system reconnaissance alerts are located in ordinary database examples. No evidence found of host enumeration, process listing, credential discovery, or environment dumping.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines occur inside repository and query examples. The semantic context is database access logic, not machine reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Database URL Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation encourages reading DATABASE_URL for production configuration. This is common, but users should keep secrets outside prompts, logs, and generated examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment access is direct and intentional. It is a managed configuration pattern, not malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw SQL Execution Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation shows a diagnostic SQL string passed to a database execute call. Users should keep raw SQL fixed, parameterized, and scoped to monitoring privileges.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The raw execution pattern is present, but the example query is static. Risk depends on how users adapt the pattern.

content.value_statement: Database code often suffers from leaks, slow queries, and fragile migrations. This skill provides practical patterns for async access, schema design, migrations, and production tuning.
actual_capabilities: Design SQLModel and SQLAlchemy models with indexes, relationships, timestamps, and soft deletion.
Configure async database engines, sessions, connection pooling, cleanup, and environment-based settings.
Create Alembic migration patterns with upgrade and downgrade flows for schema changes.
Build repository and query builder patterns for common CRUD and filtered query workflows.
Review performance patterns for slow query logging, PostgreSQL statistics, and table metrics.
Set up test database sessions and production database readiness checklists.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a runnable database package.
Examples require adaptation for each framework, database driver, and deployment environment.
Production credentials, SSL settings, and access controls must be supplied by the user.
Raw SQL and indexing advice should be reviewed against real query plans.

use_cases.title: Design a Service Database Layer
use_cases.description: Create models, repositories, and async sessions for a Python API with clear transaction handling.

use_cases.title: Improve Query Performance
use_cases.description: Identify missing indexes, review slow query patterns, and plan safer database statistics checks.

use_cases.title: Prepare Production Configuration
use_cases.description: Review pooling, backups, monitoring, SSL, and least-privilege database settings before release.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Models
prompt_templates.prompt: Create SQLModel models for [entities] with fields [fields], timestamps, primary keys, and useful indexes.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Async Sessions
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an async SQLAlchemy session setup for [database] with pooling, rollback handling, and clean shutdown.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan an Alembic migration for [schema change], including upgrade steps, downgrade steps, and data safety checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Performance Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these database access patterns for slow queries, missing indexes, unsafe raw SQL, and transaction risks: [details].

output_examples.input: Design a task management schema with users, tasks, tags, and soft deletion.
output: A normalized model plan with users, tasks, tags, and a join table.
Index recommendations for owner, status, priority, creation time, and deletion state.
Notes about timestamps, uniqueness, and relationship loading.

output_examples.input: Review my database setup before production.
output: A checklist covering pooling, SSL, backups, monitoring, and credential handling.
Warnings about copied fallback credentials and unsafe local database paths.
Suggested tests for transaction rollback and connection cleanup.

output_examples.input: Help optimize slow task search queries.
output: A query review focused on filters, joins, ordering, and pagination.
Index suggestions matched to common lookup and sorting paths.
Guidance to validate changes with real query plans.

best_practices: Keep database credentials in managed secrets and avoid fallback production passwords in copied code.
Use async sessions with explicit rollback, cleanup, and lifecycle ownership in application frameworks.
Validate indexes and raw SQL with real query plans before applying production changes.
anti_patterns: Do not paste production database URLs into prompts, logs, examples, or committed configuration.
Do not copy raw SQL examples into user-facing query paths without parameterization and review.
Do not use development SQLite file paths as production storage locations.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to my database?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and implementation patterns that you adapt in your own project.

faq.question: Which databases does it cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite through SQLModel and SQLAlchemy patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with migrations?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Alembic migration structure, upgrade steps, downgrade steps, and index creation patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe for production use?
faq.answer: The guidance can support production systems, but credentials, SSL, roles, and deployment settings need local review.

faq.question: Does it replace database tuning?
faq.answer: No. It gives review patterns, but final tuning should use real metrics, query plans, and workload data.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is marked for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.
